coredata.dc:title: Smart integration of carbon quantum dots in metal-organic frameworks for fluorescence-functionalized phase change materials
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiao
Gao, Hongyi
Yang, Mu
Xing, Liwen
Dong, Wenjun
Li, Ang
Zheng, Haiyan
Wang, Ge
coredata.dc:description: A novel type of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) based photoluminescence-functionalized (PL) phase change materials (PCMs) was designed and fabricated for superior thermal energy and fluorescence harvesting using a facile synthetic strategy for the first time, which expands conventional single thermal nature of PCMs with novel fluorescence function. Stearic acid (SA) and carbon quantum dot (CQD) molecules were synchronously incorporated into the Cr-MIL-101-NH2 framework, in which MOF framework serves as an ideal compatible support host, CQD as a superior fluorescent guest, and stearic acid as an excellent thermal energy guest. Our uniquely constructed MOF-involved PCMs assisted by CQDs effectively preclude conventional aggregation-induced fluorescent quenching during the operation. This novel targeted functional strategy creates an innovative platform for developing advanced multifunctional PCMs with multiple fascinating peculiarities and desired properties, which can be universally applied to other versatile MOF hosts and other functional guests for expanding the applications of MOF-based PCMs and developing modular devices with multiple enhanced functional properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic frameworks
Phase change materials
Carbon quantum dots
Photoluminescence function

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of photoluminescence-functionalized composite PCMs; (b) fluorescent images of pure CQDs at different excitation wavelengths, (c) HRTEM image of pure CQDs, (d) diameter distribution of pure CQDs, the insert is CQDs production, (e) UV–vis absorption spectrum and excitation-independent PL spectra of pure CQDs.
(a–c) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of Cr-MIL-101-NH2 with different CQD contents, (d–f) XPS of Cr-MIL-101-NH2 with 200 mg CQDs.

(a-c) DSC thermogram and phase change enthalpies of MOF-based composite PCMs with different CQD contents, (d) melting and freezing enthalpy of MOF-based composite PCMs (inserting 200 mg CQDs) at different cycling times, (e) DSC curves of MOF-based composite PCMs with 100 mg CQDs before and after 50 times thermal cycling, (f) fluorescence lifetime of pure CQDs, Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD support and corresponding stearic acid @ Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD PCMs (100 mg CQDs), (g-i) PL spectra of different MOF-based composite PCMs with different CQD contents, excitation wavelength: 475 nm.

(a-c) Fluorescent images of MOF-based composite PCMs with 200 mg CQDs at different excitation wavelengths, (d) corresponding device photo of SA@MOF/CQD composite PCM-encapsulated LED, (e) temperature-time curve of the device before and after infiltrating composite PCMs, (f) schematic of SA@MOF/CQD composite PCMs excited fluorescence.

head.ce:title: Smart integration of carbon quantum dots in metal-organic frameworks for fluorescence-functionalized phase change materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Materials Genome Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Function Materials for Molecule&Structure Construction, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Materials Genome Engineering, Beijing Key Laboratory of Function Materials for Molecule&Structure Construction, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel type of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) based photoluminescence-functionalized (PL) phase change materials (PCMs) was designed and fabricated for superior thermal energy and fluorescence harvesting using a facile synthetic strategy for the first time, which expands conventional single thermal nature of PCMs with novel fluorescence function. Stearic acid (SA) and carbon quantum dot (CQD) molecules were synchronously incorporated into the Cr-MIL-101-NH2 framework, in which MOF framework serves as an ideal compatible support host, CQD as a superior fluorescent guest, and stearic acid as an excellent thermal energy guest. Our uniquely constructed MOF-involved PCMs assisted by CQDs effectively preclude conventional aggregation-induced fluorescent quenching during the operation. This novel targeted functional strategy creates an innovative platform for developing advanced multifunctional PCMs with multiple fascinating peculiarities and desired properties, which can be universally applied to other versatile MOF hosts and other functional guests for expanding the applications of MOF-based PCMs and developing modular devices with multiple enhanced functional properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Phase change materials, as the main latent thermal energy storage medium, can capture excess thermal energy from their surroundings and release it via phase transition when required [1–4]. Currently, solid-liquid PCMs are predominantly taken into account in thermal energy management system due to their smaller volume evolution and less energy loss [5–8]. However, the leakage during the phase change process greatly limits the application of the solid-liquid PCMs. Diverse conventional porous materials, including zeolite, diatomite, silica, graphene, carbon nanotubes, expended graphite and porous carbons [4,9–15], are utilized as the support for the preparation of shape-stabilized PCMs to address the leakage issue. Nevertheless, the low surface area and pore volume of most conventional supports prevents the achievement of high thermal energy storage density [16–20]. Hence, ideal porous supports are sought for accessing better thermal properties. MOFs with the merits of ultrahigh surface area, high porosity, adjustable pore size and customizable chemistry have garnered increasing attention for their utilization as host matrices [21–26]. Herein, we have successfully introduced MOF as a promising support for preparing shape-stabilized composite PCMs to obtain better thermal properties.

To date, most reported PCMs are utilized for the construction of architecture thermal management, which are necessarily extended into more frontier fields for satisfying specific multifunctional requirements. As for versatile MOFs, the host-guest chemistry provides a novel platform for developing advanced multifunctional composite materials [27–31]. The multi-functionalization integration between MOF hosts with functional guests is a promising route for expanding MOF-based applications, which often shows enhanced or even unprecedented novel features compared with their pristine counterparts [26,32]. Currently, introducing fluorescence guest molecules into MOFs to construct luminescent-functionalized MOFs for light emitting diode (LED) chips have attracted considerable interest [33–36]. However, the aggregation-induced quenching of organic phosphors often emerges in their solid states, thus limiting their application [33]. Although some MOFs are luminescent, very few pristine MOFs are appropriate for preparing shape-stabilized PCMs due to the low thermal energy storage density [37–39]. Additionally, the increasing operation temperature of the LED regularly results in decreased efficiency and color instability, thus shortening its service life [40–42]. Hence, in this study, zero-dimensional light-emitting quantum-sized CQDs were selected as a superior fluorescent activity guest due to their inherent intense photoluminescence, excellent photostability, and high compatibility [43,44]. Meanwhile, incorporating PCM guest molecules into CQD-decorated MOF hosts absorbs excess thermal energy and maintain the PCM-encapsulated LED at a suitable temperature during the operation, which creates an opportunity for developing novel fluorescent PCMs and prolonging LED service life. Indeed, function-leading preparation of composite PCMs is strongly imperative, but it is still in its infancy. To the best of our knowledge, photoluminescence-functionalized PCMs have not yet been reported.

Herein, we propose a facile synthetic strategy to develop a novel type of MOF-based fluorescence-functionalized PCMs assisted by a fluorescence guest and thermal energy guest for the first time. Cr-MIL-101-NH2 serves as an ideal compatible porous support host, CQD as a superior fluorescent activity guest and stearic acid as an excellent thermal energy guest. This unique structure effectively precludes conventional aggregation-induced fluorescent quenching during the operation [45,46]. Smart integration of double superiorities of MOF and CQD guarantees the superior thermal features (high energy storage density, thermal stability and superior shape-stabilized ability) of pristine MOF-based PCMs, and further results in novel high-intensity fluorescence function with excellent color stability, thus satisfying the specific multifunctional requirements in particular devices, such as LEDs. This novel MOF-involved PL-functionalized composite PCMs strategy assisted by CQDs creates an innovative platform for developing advanced multifunctional PCMs with multiple fascinating peculiarities and desired properties.

CQDs were synthesized according to a previously reported procedure with some modifications [47]. Typically, 5 g sodium hydroxide, 100 mL acetone and 3 ml divinyl benzene are blended to yield an aldol reaction under vigorous stirring for 2 h, and afterwards the product was put in ambient air, at room temperature, and under normal atmospheric pressure for 72 h. Then 1 M dilute HCl was used to adjust the pH to be neutral. The product was centrifuged and purified with deionized water numerous times, and the ultimate product was frozen at − 70 °C for 24 h to obtain CQD powder.

The synthesis of Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD was achieved via a one-step procedure as follows: typically, H2BDC-NH2 (1.44 g, 8.0 mmol), Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (3.20 g, 8.0 mmol) and NaOH (0.80 g, 20 mmol) were dissolved in 60 mL deionized water under vigorous stirring for 30 min at room temperature. Then, different contents of CQDs were slowly dispersed, and the color of the mixture solution changed to light yellow. Subsequently, the resulting suspension was vigorously stirred for 30 min, then, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 12 h under autogenous pressure, followed by cooling to room temperature. The obtained solid product was isolated via centrifugation and washed three times with DMF, methanol and deionized water, respectively.

SA@MOF/CQD PCM composites were successfully prepared via a physical mixing and impregnation method. Prior to PCM composite synthesis, MOF/CQD support was fully activated at 80 °C via vacuum drying method. First, SA was completely melted and dissolved in the absolute ethanol for 0.5 h at 70 °C. Then, MOF/CQD support was distributed in the SA solution and the resulting mixture was vigorously stirred for 2 h at 70 °C. Finally, the solution product was evaporated at 80 °C for 24 h to yield SA@MOF/CQD composite PCMs.

The morphologies of the samples were observed by SEM (ZEISS, SUPRA55) and TEM (JEM-2010). The structure and phase were characterized using UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600) and XRD (M21X, Cu Ka radiation, at 40 kV and 150 mA, λ = 0.15406 nm). The functional groups were evaluated by FTIR (Nicolet 6700) using the KBr pellet technique. The compositions and binding energy were studied through XPS (Thermo escalab 250Xi). The specific surface area and pore volume were calculated by the BET method. The pore size distribution was derived from the adsorption branches of isotherms by using BJH model. PL spectra were obtained from a fluorescence spectrometer (FluoroMax-4). Fluorescent images were obtained using a laser scanning confocal microscope (Olympus FV-1200) at different excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 546 nm. Fluorescence lifetime was measured through a fluorescence spectrometer (F900) (excitation wavelength: 475 nm, emission wavelength: 585 nm). Thermogravimetric analysis was performed by TGA (Netzsch STA449F) under N2 flow. Thermal properties of the composite PCMs were characterized by DSC (NETZSCH STA449F3, Germany) at a heating rate of 10 °C/min and a cooling rate of 10 °C/min under 50 ml/min N2 flow. Thermal conductivity was performed by a transient plane source technique (Hot Disk TPS 2500S, Sweden) via putting two tablets containing the same kind of PCMs. Temperature-time curves were recorded using a digital R2100 data collector. The shape-stabilized ability was detected by observing the sample shape evolution with the increase of temperature, followed by taking the corresponding digital photographs.

The novel type of MOF-based PL-functionalized composite PCMs were firstly prepared by synchronously incorporating the luminescence guest and PCM guest into the MOF host ( Fig. 1a). The Cr-MIL-101-NH2 was selected as the MOF host, CQD and SA were selected as the luminescence guest and PCM guest, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1c, homogeneously dispersing CQDs were prepared via aldol reaction of acetones assisted by divinyl benzene. Under alkaline conditions, acetones can be converted into unsaturated ketones, thus forming numerous oligomers. Some side reactions, such as dehydrogenation and dehydration, would extend carbon chains due to strong alkaline condition, which may further curl and intertwine, eventually yielding CQDs [48,49]. Divinyl benzene effectively accelerates aldol reaction of acetones. Notably, the resulting product is productive (Fig. 1d).

TEM images reveal that as-prepared CQDs are monodisperse with diameters mainly ranging from 2.0 to 4.0 nm (Fig. 1c-d). The average size of as-prepared CQDs is ~ 2.7 nm. The carbon bonding configurations and elemental composition of as-prepared CQDs were measured by XPS (Fig. S3), revealing that CQDs contain rich oxygenous functional groups, such as C-OH and C=O. In the high-resolution C1s spectra, the peaks at binding energies of 284.8, 286.7, and 288.9 eV are attributed to sp2 carbon atoms, alcoholic (C-OH) and carbonyl (C=O) carbon atoms, respectively [50]. The sp2 carbon atoms might be more beneficial to thermal properties of composite PCMs. Meanwhile, the functional groups were further detected by FTIR (Fig. S3), indicating the stretching vibrations of C-OH (3435 cm−1), C-H (2960 and 2870 cm−1), C=O (1710 cm−1), and C=C (1660 cm−1), in accordance with the XPS result. These rich oxygenous functional groups might be in favor of their insertion and combination with Cr-MIL-101-NH2, due to existing hydrogen bonding. The connected chemical groups contribute to the absorption of as-prepared CQDs at the UV–vis regions. The as-prepared CQDs exhibit two characteristic UV–vis absorption peaks at 248 nm and 310 nm (Fig. 1e), which is associated with the π-π* (aromatic ring structure) and n-π* (C=O) electronic transition, respectively [51,52]. Fig. 1e depicts the high-intensity fluorescent emission spectra of as-prepared CQDs at diverse excitation wavelengths, thus revealing their excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior. When the excitation wavelengths are evolved from 375 to 500 nm, all the PL emission peaks are located at 586 nm and the PL intensities exhibit an enhanced trend. The excitation-independent fluorescent emission spectra indicate high homogeneity of CQDs with a relatively uniform and well-passivated surface state. This phenomenon may be attributed to the optical selection by the sizes, emissive sites and surface defects of CQDs [53,54]. Fluorescent images (Fig. 1b) reveal that as-prepared CQDs can emit high-intensity red-green-blue fluorescence due to high electron-hole recombination at different excitation wavelengths of 405, 488, and 546 nm, respectively, indicating their multicolor fluorescence.

The Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite support was prepared via hydrothermal method. After introducing diverse contents of CQDs into Cr-MIL-101-NH2, no obvious new peaks (Fig. S5) emerged in the XRD patterns except two main diffraction peaks positions at 9.1 and 17.3° of pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 [55], implying high phase stability of the MOF framework. FTIR spectra (Fig. S4) of the Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite support shows the stretching vibration of N-H at 667 cm−1, C-N at 1581 cm−1, C=O at 1651 cm−1, C-H at 2870 and 2968 cm−1, and C-OH at 3426 cm−1 [17]. Most functional groups of pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 are maintained, further indicating high structural stability of Cr-MIL-101-NH2 framework. Importantly, the stretching vibration of C=O group of SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD is slightly altered, indicating the formation of a new intermolecular hydrogen bonding between the amino MOFs support and carboxylic acid group [56]. Pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 and Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite support are further detected by fluorescence spectra under excitation wavelengths of 405, 488 and 546 nm, respectively (Fig. S7). The pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 support only emits weak blue fluorescence, while high-intensity red-green-blue multicolor fluorescence emerges when introducing the CQD guest into the pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 host, indicating the excellent compatibility between CQDs and Cr-MIL-101-NH2 without fluorescence quenching. Moreover, fluorescence behavior is still reserved whether stearic acid, octadecanol or polyethylene glycol is infiltrated into the Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD support (
Fig. 3g–i). The tunable fluorescence emission of Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite support is synchronously controlled by the domain size of the sp2 conjugation, chemical functional groups and surface energy traps [57,58]. Actually, in order to compare with the one-pot procedure, we prepared a Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite by physically impregnating CQDs into Cr-MIL-101-NH2 framework. Resultantly, the Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite by direct impregnation demonstrates obvious aggregation phenomenon of CQDs (Fig. S18), which influences the fluorescence performance and potential application. The obtained Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite through one-pot procedure shows relatively better uniform dispersion of CQDs in the Cr-MIL-101-NH2 framework (Fig. S7). The relatively uniformly dispersed CQDs in the Cr-MIL-101-NH2 framework further guarantee better fluorescence emission.

Pore structure is crucial to the thermal storage capacity of composite PCMs. The PCM molecular motion might be obstructed if the mean pore diameter is too small, thus resulting in low latent thermal storage capacity [17]. Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms for pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 and corresponding composite supports were measured (Fig. 2a–c and Table S1). Pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 shows a type-I N2 adsorption isotherm with mean pore diameter of 3.70 nm and high specific surface area of 1490.49 m2/g. After incorporating CQDs into the Cr-MIL-101-NH2, all the samples exhibit relatively reduced specific surface area and pore volume, due to the occupation of CQDs inside MOF pores. Based on the size distribution of as-prepared CQDs (mainly 1.5–4.0 nm), it can be concluded that the CQDs are inserted into partial micropores and mesopores of the MOF support. The average pore size of Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite support is large enough for stearic acid molecules to rotate freely because the molecular length of stearic acid is ~ 2.0 nm [59]. Hence, our regulated channels guarantee high storage capacity of stearic acid and stabilize stearic acid in the Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD matrix due to the sufficient capillary force.

XPS (Fig. 2d–f, Fig. S6) is performed to analyze elemental composition and bonding configurations of pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 and CQD-decorated Cr-MIL-101-NH2 composite supports, indicating the presence of abundant oxygen functional groups. The C1s, N1s and Cr2p peaks of pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 and CQD-decorated Cr-MIL-101-NH2 composite supports almost remain their consistency without the evolution of peak species, revealing the excellent structural stability of the MOF support. In the C1s spectra, the peaks at 284.6, 286.2, and 288.8 eV are ascribed to the sp2 hybridized (C-C), alcoholic (C-OH) and carbonyl (C=O) carbon atoms [50], respectively, in accordance with the FTIR result (Fig. S9). More significantly, after incorporating stearic acid inside the porous Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite support, C1s, N1s and Cr2p peaks (Fig. S12) are still retained, indicating the superior stability of MOF-based composite support.

Thermal stability is an essential indicator in the development and application of composite PCMs, which were measured by TGA (Fig. S14) from room temperature to 900 °C in a N2 atmosphere. The starting pyrolysis temperature of pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 is about 100 °C, which can be ascribed to the rapid evaporation of water molecules. As the temperature gradually increases, the Cr-MIL-101-NH2 framework starts to decompose until the ultimate residual weight remains 35 wt% at 900 °C. The starting pyrolysis temperature and finishing temperature of pure stearic acid is about 200 and 400 °C, respectively. Significantly, the finishing pyrolysis temperature of SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite PCM is approximately 600 °C. More attractively, the introduction of stearic acid molecules enhances the thermal stability of Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite support. For all the samples, there is no decomposition phenomenon below 150 °C, which is located within the range of phase transition temperature and guarantees the operation stability of the ordinary LED. This observation indicates that Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD is a feasible support for a low-moderate temperature composite PCMs. It is worth mentioning that the 70 wt%SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2 composite PCMs retain only 10% residual weight at 900 °C, due to the 65% pyrolysis loss of pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2 and 100% pyrolysis loss of pure stearic acid. Similar results occur with the 70 wt%SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite PCMs. Additionally, our constructed composite PCMs also exhibit excellent thermal cycling stability and shape-stabilized ability (Fig. S15, S17). Thermal analysis indicates that SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite PCMs are appropriate for utilization at low-moderate temperature thermal energy storage.

Thermal properties of MOF-based composite PCMs were measured by means of DSC with detailed characteristic data summarized in Table S2. Thermal enthalpy is the most reliable indicator for evaluating the thermal energy storage capacity of the composite PCMs. The fusion and solidification enthalpies (△H m and △H f) of pure stearic acid are 203.9 and 201.0 J/g, respectively. It must be accepted that amino MOFs can provide a hydrogen bond interaction site [17]. Therefore, the amino functional group of Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD support was essential for the high loading content of stearic acid due to hydrogen bonding interaction between stearic acid and amino group. When stearic acid is inserted into the MOF support, there is no leakage phenomenon in the composite PCMs due to the amino functional groups adsorbing and stabilizing the stearic acid inside the MOF support via hydrogen bonding interactions. To study the influence of CQDs on the enthalpy of composite PCMs, the loading content of stearic acid is uniformly determined to be 70 wt%. As seen in Fig. 3a-c and Table S2, the composite PCMs exhibit a high thermal energy storage density, thus satisfying the basic requirement. Interestingly, when the loading content is the same, the enthalpy of the composite PCMs becomes larger with increasing CQD content, indicating that the inserting CQDs contribute to the release of more latent heat of the composite PCMs. This leads to the enhancement of latent fusion enthalpy from 122.78 to 131.96 J/g, which is higher than that of pristine Cr-MIL-101-NH2-based PCM of 118.48 J/g. The actual △H m and △H f values of the composite PCMs are progressively approaching the theoretical △H m and △H f with increasing CQD content. Our groups have confirmed that the hydrogen bonds between SA and Cr-MIL-101-NH2 help adsorb more SA and increase the loading content [17]. However, to some extent, the stronger hydrogen bonds also restrict the free stretching and crystallization of SA chains, thus resulting in lower latent heat compared with the theoretical value [60]. In this study, the inserting CQDs will weaken the hydrogen bonds between SA and Cr-MIL-101-NH2 and drive SA more capable free stretching and crystallization, thus increasing the latent heat of SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite PCMs. With increasing CQD content, the hydrogen bond interaction between SA and Cr-MIL-101-NH2 is gradually weakened. Therefore, inserting relatively more CQDs contribute to the increased release of latent heat of the composite PCMs, which is gradually approaching the theoretical latent heat. Additionally, there is no significant evolution to the melting and freezing enthalpy of PCMs after different cycling times in Fig. 3d–e, revealing high thermal durability. These results indicate that Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD is a very promising support for superior thermal energy storage with better thermal properties.

Aside from the tempting thermal features, our constructed composite PCMs also emit multicolor fluorescence derived from CQDs. There are some synergies between SA and CQDs for both thermal and fluorescent properties, not just a simple sum. Firstly, long time operation of single CQDs regularly causes color instability at high temperature. SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite PCMs not only can still emit high-intensity fluorescence but also enhance the color stability via SA absorbing the generated heat. Secondly, pristine SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2 composite PCMs have a relatively low thermal conductivity of 0.26 W m-1 K-1. SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite PCMs raise the thermal conductivity to 0.41 W m-1 K-1 with increasing CQD content up to 300 mg (Fig. S16). The composite PCMs with relatively higher thermal conductivity show greater potential in the thermal management applications. PL spectra (Fig. 3g) reveal that the SA@Cr-MIL-101-NH2/CQD composite PCMs can emit high-intensity fluorescence without fluorescence quenching, indicating their excellent compatibility. MOF framework, as an antenna, can efficiently transfer energy to the incorporated CQD fluorophore [61]. Also, corresponding fluorescent images ( Fig. 4a–c) show CQD-decorated MOF-based composite PCMs can emit red-green-blue multicolor fluorescence while pristine MOF-based PCMs only emit weak blue fluorescence, indicating their application possibility. This phenomenon is closely associated with electron-hole recombination induced by quantum size effect, surface functional groups and surface energy traps [57,62,63]. Meanwhile, compared with pure CQDs, fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 3f, Fig. S13) demonstrates no apparent evolution when MOF support and stearic acid are introduced, indicating fluorescence stability of composite PCMs. Fig. 3g–i reveals that fluorescence properties still exist whether stearic acid, octadecanol or polyethylene glycol are inserted, indicating their universal application. Fluorescence data sufficiently specify that the CQDs are very stable inside MOF-based PCMs and still retain the high luminescent properties.

When current LED works, about 80% electrical energy is converted to thermal energy, while only 20% is converted to light, according to the relationship of light emission and junction temperature of Cree XLamp 7090XR-E [64]. The ambient temperature and the luminous rate is inversely proportional. If the excess heat can not timely dissipate, the higher temperature will affect its service life or even damage the LED permanently. The LED life is directly related to the junction temperature. The luminescence decreases with increasing junction temperature due to the low photoelectric conversion rate [65]. Typically, when the service temperature of the ordinary LED light exceeds 90 °C, its service life will deteriorates rapidly [66]. Hence, a primary challenge is maintaining the LED's junction temperature within the recommended operational range. Our obtained PL-functionalized composite PCMs not only emit high-intensity red-green-blue multicolor fluorescence, but also control the operating temperature of the ordinary PCM-encapsulated LED light via infiltrating stearic acid, thus prolonging its service life. The encapsulated LED device was designed via CQD-decorated MOF-based PCMs for evaluating its luminescent properties. As shown in Fig. 4d, the device can emit high-intensity red-green-blue fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths due to high electron-hole recombination. This result is highly consistent with the fluorescent images (Fig. 4a–c). As shown in Fig. 4e, when exceeding 60 °C, compared with the original device, the heating rate of the device after infiltrating PCMs is effectively decelerated via absorbing long time operation induced excessive heat. This targeted function strategy can be universal to other versatile MOF hosts and other functional guests for expanding the applications of MOF-based PCMs and developing modular devices with multiple enhanced functional properties.

A novel functional type of MOF-based photoluminescence-functionalized PCMs have been designed for superior thermal energy harvesting for the first time, thus expanding their conventional single thermal nature to novel fluorescence function and satisfying the specific multifunctional requirements in particular devices. Amino-functionalized MOFs with high specific surface area and pore volume guarantee excellent adsorption capacity for stearic acid due to the strong hydrogen bonds. CQD, as a type of superior fluorescent guest, can emit high-intensity polychromic fluorescence in the MOF support without fluorescence quenching. Additionally, our uniquely constructed composite PCMs exhibit excellent thermal stability, durability, and shape-stabilized ability. Such novel CQD-decorated MOF-involved composite PCMs strategy opens up an innovative avenue for developing advanced multifunctional PCMs, which can be universal to other versatile MOF hosts and other functional guests for expanding the applications of MOF-based PCMs and developing modular devices with multiple enhanced functional properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51436001 and 51702013) and Beijing Natural Science Foundation (No. 2174078).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2018.08.015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: SAE Int. J. Passeng. Cars-Mech. Syst.
